questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 8 — Gravitation, Section 8.5–8.6
questions.ncert_line: The gravitational potential energy at distance r from the Earth's centre is U = −GMm/r. The escape velocity from Earth's surface is ve = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The orbital velocity of a satellite close to Earth's surface is approximately:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 8 — Gravitation, Section 8.7: Earth Satellites
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 8 — Gravitation, Section 8.7: Earth Satellites
questions.ncert_line: For a satellite in a circular orbit at height h above Earth's surface: orbital velocity v₀ = √(GM/(R+h)). The time period T = 2π(R+h)/v₀. For h << R, v₀ ≈ √(gR) ≈ 7.9 km/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is INCORRECT about a geostationary satellite?

options.B: It orbits in the equatorial plane
options.C: It can be at any altitude above the Earth
options.D: It appears stationary relative to a point on Earth

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 8 — Gravitation, Section 8.7: Earth Satellites
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 8 — Gravitation, Section 8.7: Earth Satellites
questions.ncert_line: For a satellite in a circular orbit at height h above Earth's surface: orbital velocity v₀ = √(GM/(R+h)). The time period T = 2π(R+h)/v₀. For h << R, v₀ ≈ √(gR) ≈ 7.9 km/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Young's modulus of elasticity is defined as:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 9 — Mechanical Properties of Solids, Section 9.3–9.6
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 9 — Mechanical Properties of Solids, Section 9.3–9.6
questions.ncert_line: Young's modulus Y = (F/A)/(ΔL/L) = stress/strain. Bulk modulus B = −V(ΔP/ΔV). Shear modulus (rigidity modulus) G = shear stress/shear strain. For a material: Y > B for most metals.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble of radius r and surface tension S is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids, Section 10.7: Surface Tension
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids, Section 10.7: Surface Tension
questions.ncert_line: Surface tension (S) is the force per unit length acting tangentially on the surface of a liquid. The excess pressure inside a soap bubble is 4S/r (two surfaces). Inside a liquid drop it is 2S/r.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Bernoulli's principle is based on which conservation law?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids, Section 10.4–10.6
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids, Section 10.4–10.6
questions.ncert_line: Bernoulli's principle: For a streamlined flow, P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant. Pascal's law: Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. Archimedes' principle: upthrust = weight of fluid displaced.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For an adiabatic process, which of the following is true?

options.A: Q = 0, so ΔU = −W (work done by gas)

questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.5–12.10
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.5–12.10
questions.ncert_line: First law: ΔU = Q − W (heat absorbed = increase in internal energy + work done by gas). For an adiabatic process, Q = 0, so ΔU = −W. Entropy increases in irreversible processes (Second law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between source temperature T₁ = 800 K and sink temperature T₂ = 400 K is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.9: Carnot Engine
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.9: Carnot Engine
questions.ncert_line: The efficiency of a Carnot engine η = 1 − T₂/T₁, where T₁ is the temperature of the source and T₂ is the temperature of the sink. No engine can be more efficient than a Carnot engine operating between the same temperatures.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: No heat engine operating between two temperatures can be more efficient than a Carnot engine operating between the same temperatures.
Statement II: The efficiency of a Carnot engine can be 100% if T₂ = 0 K.

options.B: Statement I is true but Statement II is practically impossible (Third Law prevents T₂ = 0)
options.C: Statement I is false but Statement II is true

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.9: Carnot Engine
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.9: Carnot Engine
questions.ncert_line: The efficiency of a Carnot engine η = 1 − T₂/T₁, where T₁ is the temperature of the source and T₂ is the temperature of the sink. No engine can be more efficient than a Carnot engine operating between the same temperatures.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The average kinetic energy per molecule of an ideal gas is related to absolute temperature T by:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 13 — Kinetic Theory, Section 13.4–13.5
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 13 — Kinetic Theory, Section 13.4–13.5
questions.ncert_line: From kinetic theory: PV = (1/3)Nmv²rms. The average kinetic energy per molecule = (3/2)kBT. The rms speed vrms = √(3RT/M) = √(3kBT/m). For a monoatomic ideal gas, Cv = (3/2)R and Cp = (5/2)R.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The number of degrees of freedom of a diatomic molecule at room temperature is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 13 — Kinetic Theory, Section 13.6: Law of Equipartition
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 13 — Kinetic Theory, Section 13.6: Law of Equipartition
questions.ncert_line: The law of equipartition of energy: each degree of freedom contributes ½kBT to the average energy. A monoatomic gas has 3 translational degrees of freedom; diatomic gas has 5 (3 translational + 2 rotational) at room temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The ratio Cp/Cv (γ) for a monoatomic ideal gas is:
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 13 — Kinetic Theory, Section 13.6: Law of Equipartition
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 13 — Kinetic Theory, Section 13.6: Law of Equipartition
questions.ncert_line: The law of equipartition of energy: each degree of freedom contributes ½kBT to the average energy. A monoatomic gas has 3 translational degrees of freedom; diatomic gas has 5 (3 translational + 2 rotational) at room temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Water has maximum density at:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 11 — Thermal Properties of Matter, Section 11.3: Thermal Expansion
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 11 — Thermal Properties of Matter, Section 11.3: Thermal Expansion
questions.ncert_line: Coefficient of linear expansion α: ΔL = L₀αΔT. Coefficient of volume expansion γ = 3α. Water shows anomalous expansion — it expands on cooling below 4°C and has maximum density at 4°C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In simple harmonic motion, the restoring force is:

options.A: Constant and directed toward mean position
options.B: Proportional to displacement and directed toward mean position
options.D: Proportional to displacement² and directed away from mean

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 14 — Oscillations, Section 14.3–14.4
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 14 — Oscillations, Section 14.3–14.4
questions.ncert_line: In SHM, the restoring force F = −kx (proportional to displacement, directed toward mean position). Displacement x = A cos(ωt + φ). Angular frequency ω = √(k/m) for a spring-mass system. Period T = 2π/ω = 2π√(m/k).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following does NOT affect the time period of a simple pendulum?

options.D: Both C (mass) and the amplitude for small oscillations

questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 14 — Oscillations, Section 14.5: The Simple Pendulum
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 14 — Oscillations, Section 14.5: The Simple Pendulum
questions.ncert_line: The time period of a simple pendulum: T = 2π√(L/g). It is independent of the mass of the bob and the amplitude (for small oscillations). T increases if g decreases (e.g., at higher altitude or in a lift accelerating downward).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The speed of a transverse wave on a string depends on:

options.C: Tension in the string and linear mass density

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 15 — Waves, Section 15.2–15.4
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 15 — Waves, Section 15.2–15.4
questions.ncert_line: For a transverse wave y = A sin(kx − ωt + φ). Wave speed v = ω/k = λf. For a string: v = √(T/μ), where T is tension and μ is linear mass density. Speed of sound in air increases with temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two tuning forks of frequencies 512 Hz and 516 Hz are sounded simultaneously. The number of beats per second is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 15 — Waves, Section 15.7: Beats
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 15 — Waves, Section 15.7: Beats
questions.ncert_line: When two sources of slightly different frequencies f₁ and f₂ are superimposed, beats are heard. Beat frequency = |f₁ − f₂|. Maximum beats per second that the human ear can distinguish is about 10.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A source of sound moves toward a stationary observer. The observer will hear a sound of:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 15 — Waves, Section 15.9: Doppler Effect
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 15 — Waves, Section 15.9: Doppler Effect
questions.ncert_line: Doppler effect: The apparent frequency of a wave changes when source or observer is in motion. When source and observer approach each other, apparent frequency increases. Formula: f' = f₀(v + v₀)/(v − vs).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The electric field inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 1 — Electric Charges and Fields, Section 1.8: Gauss's Law
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 1 — Electric Charges and Fields, Section 1.8: Gauss's Law
questions.ncert_line: Gauss's law: The total electric flux through a closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed divided by ε₀: φ = Q_enc/ε₀. The electric field inside a conductor is zero; charge resides only on the surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has capacitance C. If a dielectric slab (K = 3) completely fills the space, the new capacitance is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 2 — Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance, Section 2.9–2.10
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 2 — Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance, Section 2.9–2.10
questions.ncert_line: Capacitance C = Q/V. For a parallel plate capacitor C = ε₀A/d (no dielectric) or C = Kε₀A/d (with dielectric). Energy stored in a capacitor U = Q²/2C = CV²/2 = QV/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A capacitor is charged and then DISCONNECTED from the battery. A dielectric is inserted between its plates. Which of the following is CORRECT?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 2 — Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance, Section 2.10.2: Effect of Dielectric
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 2 — Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance, Section 2.10.2: Effect of Dielectric
questions.ncert_line: When a dielectric is inserted between the capacitor plates (capacitor disconnected from battery), the capacitance increases by factor K but charge remains constant, so voltage decreases and energy stored decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C charged to voltage V is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 2 — Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance, Section 2.9–2.10
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 2 — Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance, Section 2.9–2.10
questions.ncert_line: Capacitance C = Q/V. For a parallel plate capacitor C = ε₀A/d (no dielectric) or C = Kε₀A/d (with dielectric). Energy stored in a capacitor U = Q²/2C = CV²/2 = QV/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The resistivity of a metallic conductor generally:

options.D: First decreases then increases with temperature

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.5: Drift of Electrons
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.5: Drift of Electrons
questions.ncert_line: Drift velocity vd = eEτ/m = I/(nAe), where n is number density of electrons, A is cross-sectional area and e is electron charge. Resistivity ρ = m/(ne²τ), where τ is relaxation time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is of the order of:
questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.5: Drift of Electrons
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.5: Drift of Electrons
questions.ncert_line: Drift velocity vd = eEτ/m = I/(nAe), where n is number density of electrons, A is cross-sectional area and e is electron charge. Resistivity ρ = m/(ne²τ), where τ is relaxation time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a Wheatstone bridge, no current flows through the galvanometer when:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.14–3.15
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.14–3.15
questions.ncert_line: The Wheatstone bridge is balanced (no current through galvanometer) when P/Q = R/S. The metre bridge is a practical application. The potentiometer is used for accurate measurement of EMF and has no current drawn from the cell under balanced condition.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: A potentiometer can be used to compare the EMFs of two cells without drawing any current from them at the balance point.
Statement II: At the balance point in a potentiometer, the galvanometer shows zero deflection.

options.A: Both are true and Statement II explains Statement I
options.B: Both are true but Statement II does NOT explain Statement I
options.C: Statement I is true but Statement II is false

questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.10: Kirchhoff's Laws
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.10: Kirchhoff's Laws
questions.ncert_line: Kirchhoff's current law (KCL): The algebraic sum of currents at any junction is zero. Kirchhoff's voltage law (KVL): The algebraic sum of potential differences in any closed loop is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is INCORRECT about the magnetic force on a moving charge?

options.A: It is always perpendicular to the velocity
options.B: It does positive work on the charge
options.C: It cannot change the speed of the charge
options.D: Its direction is given by F = qv × B

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4 — Moving Charges and Magnetism, Section 4.2: Magnetic Force
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4 — Moving Charges and Magnetism, Section 4.2: Magnetic Force
questions.ncert_line: The magnetic force on a moving charge: F = qv × B = qvB sinθ. This force is always perpendicular to both v and B, so it does no work on the charge and cannot change kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The cyclotron frequency (f = qB/2πm) is independent of:
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4 — Moving Charges and Magnetism, Section 4.5: Cyclotron
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4 — Moving Charges and Magnetism, Section 4.5: Cyclotron
questions.ncert_line: In a cyclotron, the radius of circular motion r = mv/qB. The cyclotron frequency f = qB/2πm — it is independent of the speed and radius (for non-relativistic speeds). Cyclotrons cannot accelerate electrons (too light, quickly become relativistic).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The magnetic field inside a long solenoid carrying current I with n turns per unit length is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4 — Moving Charges and Magnetism, Section 4.7: Ampere's Law
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4 — Moving Charges and Magnetism, Section 4.7: Ampere's Law
questions.ncert_line: Ampere's circuital law: ∮B·dl = μ₀I_enc. The field inside a long solenoid: B = μ₀nI, where n is the number of turns per unit length. Outside a solenoid, B = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: According to Lenz's law, the induced current in a loop:

options.A: Aids the change in flux that produces it
options.B: Opposes the change in flux that produces it
options.C: Is always in the clockwise direction
options.D: Is proportional to the rate of change of current

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 6 — Electromagnetic Induction, Section 6.3–6.4
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 6 — Electromagnetic Induction, Section 6.3–6.4
questions.ncert_line: Faraday's law: The induced EMF in a loop is ε = −dΦ/dt, where Φ = BA cosθ is the magnetic flux. Lenz's law: The induced current opposes the change that produces it (the negative sign in Faraday's law).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The energy stored in an inductor of inductance L carrying current I is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 6 — Electromagnetic Induction, Section 6.7–6.8
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 6 — Electromagnetic Induction, Section 6.7–6.8
questions.ncert_line: Self-inductance L is defined by ε = −L(dI/dt). Energy stored in an inductor: U = ½LI². For a solenoid: L = μ₀n²V, where V is volume. Mutual inductance M: ε₂ = −M(dI₁/dt).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In a series LCR circuit at resonance, the impedance is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 7 — Alternating Current, Section 7.5–7.8
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 7 — Alternating Current, Section 7.5–7.8
questions.ncert_line: In an LCR series circuit, impedance Z = √(R² + (XL − XC)²). At resonance, XL = XC, so Z = R (minimum) and current is maximum. Resonant frequency f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)). Quality factor Q = ω₀L/R.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following is INCORRECT about a transformer?

options.A: It works on the principle of mutual induction
options.B: It can work with both AC and DC
options.C: In a step-up transformer, Vs > Vp
options.D: An ideal transformer conserves power (Pin = Pout)

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 7 — Alternating Current, Section 7.10: Transformer
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 7 — Alternating Current, Section 7.10: Transformer
questions.ncert_line: For an ideal transformer: Vs/Vp = Ns/Np = Ip/Is. A step-up transformer increases voltage but decreases current proportionally (power is conserved). Transformers work only with AC, not DC.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following EM waves has the highest frequency?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8 — Electromagnetic Waves, Section 8.3: Electromagnetic Spectrum
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8 — Electromagnetic Waves, Section 8.3: Electromagnetic Spectrum
questions.ncert_line: The electromagnetic spectrum in increasing order of frequency (decreasing wavelength): Radio waves < Microwaves < Infrared < Visible < Ultraviolet < X-rays < Gamma rays. Gamma rays are produced in nuclear transitions; X-rays by bombarding metal targets with high-energy electrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the production of X-rays?

options.B: Bombardment of a metal target with high-energy electrons
options.C: Electron transitions in the outer shells of atoms

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8 — Electromagnetic Waves, Section 8.3: Electromagnetic Spectrum
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8 — Electromagnetic Waves, Section 8.3: Electromagnetic Spectrum
questions.ncert_line: The electromagnetic spectrum in increasing order of frequency (decreasing wavelength): Radio waves < Microwaves < Infrared < Visible < Ultraviolet < X-rays < Gamma rays. Gamma rays are produced in nuclear transitions; X-rays by bombarding metal targets with high-energy electrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Maxwell's displacement current arises due to:

options.A: Flow of free electrons in a vacuum
options.B: A changing electric field between capacitor plates

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8 — Electromagnetic Waves, Section 8.2: Displacement Current
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8 — Electromagnetic Waves, Section 8.2: Displacement Current
questions.ncert_line: Maxwell added the concept of displacement current (ε₀ dΦE/dt) to Ampere's law to make it consistent. Displacement current arises due to changing electric field and produces a magnetic field just like ordinary conduction current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Total internal reflection can occur when light travels from:

options.A: A rarer to a denser medium
options.B: A denser to a rarer medium, at angles greater than the critical angle
options.D: A medium of n=1.5 to a medium of n=2.0

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 9 — Ray Optics, Section 9.3: Refraction
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 9 — Ray Optics, Section 9.3: Refraction
questions.ncert_line: Snell's law: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂. Refractive index n = c/v = sin i/sin r. Total internal reflection occurs when the angle of incidence in the denser medium exceeds the critical angle θc = sin⁻¹(n₂/n₁).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two thin lenses of focal lengths f₁ = 20 cm and f₂ = −30 cm are placed in contact. The equivalent focal length is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 9 — Ray Optics, Section 9.6: Thin Lenses
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 9 — Ray Optics, Section 9.6: Thin Lenses
questions.ncert_line: Lens formula: 1/v − 1/u = 1/f. Lensmaker's equation: 1/f = (n−1)(1/R₁ − 1/R₂). Power P = 1/f (in dioptres when f is in metres). For thin lenses in contact: 1/f = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When white light passes through a glass prism, which colour is deviated the MOST?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 9 — Ray Optics, Section 9.7: Dispersion by a Prism
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 9 — Ray Optics, Section 9.7: Dispersion by a Prism
questions.ncert_line: Dispersion is the splitting of white light into its component colours by a prism. Violet light is deviated most (highest refractive index, lowest speed) and red light least. The angle of minimum deviation δm = A(n−1) for small angles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Young's double slit experiment, if the slit separation d is doubled keeping D and λ constant, the fringe width β will:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.3: Young's Double Slit
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.3: Young's Double Slit
questions.ncert_line: In Young's double slit experiment, fringe width β = λD/d, where λ is wavelength, D is slit-to-screen distance, and d is slit separation. Constructive interference (bright fringe): path difference = nλ. Destructive: (n + ½)λ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Young's double slit experiment, the fringe width β = λD/d. If λ = 600 nm, D = 1 m, and d = 0.2 mm, the fringe width is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.3: Young's Double Slit
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.3: Young's Double Slit
questions.ncert_line: In Young's double slit experiment, fringe width β = λD/d, where λ is wavelength, D is slit-to-screen distance, and d is slit separation. Constructive interference (bright fringe): path difference = nλ. Destructive: (n + ½)λ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: According to Malus's law, when plane polarised light (intensity I₀) passes through an analyser at 60° to the polariser, the transmitted intensity is:
questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.6: Polarisation
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.6: Polarisation
questions.ncert_line: Malus's law: When plane polarised light passes through an analyser, the intensity I = I₀ cos²θ, where θ is the angle between the polariser and analyser axes. Polarisation proves the transverse nature of light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In single slit diffraction, if the slit width is decreased, the central maximum will:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.5: Diffraction
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.5: Diffraction
questions.ncert_line: In single slit diffraction, the condition for the first dark fringe is a sinθ = λ, where a is the slit width. The central maximum is twice as wide as secondary maxima. Narrower the slit, wider the central maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the photoelectric effect, which of the following statements is INCORRECT?

options.A: Increasing the intensity increases the number of emitted electrons
options.B: Kinetic energy of photoelectrons increases with frequency of light
options.C: There is a minimum threshold frequency below which no emission occurs
options.D: Increasing intensity of light increases the maximum KE of photoelectrons

questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 11 — Dual Nature of Radiation, Section 11.4: Photoelectric Effect
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 11 — Dual Nature of Radiation, Section 11.4: Photoelectric Effect
questions.ncert_line: The work function (φ) is the minimum energy needed to eject a photoelectron from a metal surface. Threshold frequency ν₀ = φ/h. The stopping potential V₀ is related to maximum KE by eV₀ = hν − φ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An electron is accelerated through potential V. Its de Broglie wavelength λ is proportional to:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 11 — Dual Nature of Radiation, Section 11.5: de Broglie Hypothesis
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 11 — Dual Nature of Radiation, Section 11.5: de Broglie Hypothesis
questions.ncert_line: de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through potential V: λ = h/√(2meV) = 1.227/√V nm. For a particle of momentum p: λ = h/p = h/mv.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In the Bohr model of hydrogen, the radius of the nth orbit is proportional to:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 12 — Atoms, Section 12.2: Bohr Model
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 12 — Atoms, Section 12.2: Bohr Model
questions.ncert_line: In the Bohr model, the radius of nth orbit: rn = n²a₀ where a₀ = 0.529 Å (Bohr radius). Energy of nth orbit: En = −13.6/n² eV. The frequency of emitted radiation: hν = En₁ − En₂.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum lies in which region of the electromagnetic spectrum?
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 12 — Atoms, Section 12.4: Hydrogen Spectrum
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 12 — Atoms, Section 12.4: Hydrogen Spectrum
questions.ncert_line: Hydrogen spectrum series: Lyman series (UV, n→1), Balmer series (visible, n→2), Paschen series (IR, n→3), Brackett (IR, n→4), Pfund (IR, n→5). The Rydberg formula: 1/λ = R(1/n₁² − 1/n₂²).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A photon is emitted when a hydrogen atom transitions from n=3 to n=2. This emission corresponds to which spectral series?
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 12 — Atoms, Section 12.4: Hydrogen Spectrum
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 12 — Atoms, Section 12.4: Hydrogen Spectrum
questions.ncert_line: Hydrogen spectrum series: Lyman series (UV, n→1), Balmer series (visible, n→2), Paschen series (IR, n→3), Brackett (IR, n→4), Pfund (IR, n→5). The Rydberg formula: 1/λ = R(1/n₁² − 1/n₂²).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The binding energy per nucleon is maximum for elements with mass number around:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.3: Binding Energy
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.3: Binding Energy
questions.ncert_line: Binding energy per nucleon is maximum (~8.8 MeV) around mass number A ≈ 56 (iron group). Elements lighter than iron gain energy by fusion; those heavier than iron gain energy by fission. Mass defect Δm = Zmp + (A−Z)mn − M(nucleus).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In beta-minus decay, the atomic number Z of the nucleus:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.4: Radioactivity
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.4: Radioactivity
questions.ncert_line: Radioactive decay law: N = N₀e^(−λt). Activity A = λN. Half-life T₁/₂ = 0.693/λ. In alpha decay, A decreases by 4 and Z decreases by 2. In beta-minus decay, Z increases by 1 and A is unchanged. In gamma decay, only energy is released.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The half-life of a radioactive sample is 30 minutes. After 2 hours, the fraction of the original sample remaining is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.4: Radioactivity
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.4: Radioactivity
questions.ncert_line: Radioactive decay law: N = N₀e^(−λt). Activity A = λN. Half-life T₁/₂ = 0.693/λ. In alpha decay, A decreases by 4 and Z decreases by 2. In beta-minus decay, Z increases by 1 and A is unchanged. In gamma decay, only energy is released.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question:
Statement I: Nuclear fission of U-235 releases about 200 MeV per fission event.
Statement II: Nuclear fusion releases more energy per unit mass than fission.

options.B: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
options.C: Statement I is false but Statement II is true

questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.5–13.6: Fission and Fusion
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.5–13.6: Fission and Fusion
questions.ncert_line: Nuclear fission: A heavy nucleus (e.g., U-235) splits into two medium-sized nuclei on neutron absorption, releasing ~200 MeV of energy and 2-3 neutrons. Nuclear fusion requires extremely high temperature (≥10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb repulsion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In an n-type semiconductor, the majority charge carriers are:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.4–14.5
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.4–14.5
questions.ncert_line: In intrinsic semiconductors, electrons and holes are always equal (ne = nh). In n-type (donor impurity), electrons are majority carriers. In p-type (acceptor impurity), holes are majority carriers. At 0 K, semiconductors behave as insulators.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In forward bias of a p-n junction, the depletion region:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.6: p-n Junction
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.6: p-n Junction
questions.ncert_line: In a p-n junction diode, forward bias reduces the depletion region and allows current flow. Reverse bias widens the depletion region and allows only a tiny reverse (leakage) current. A diode acts as a rectifier — it conducts in one direction only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A Zener diode is used primarily as a:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.7.2: Zener Diode
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.7.2: Zener Diode
questions.ncert_line: A Zener diode is a heavily doped p-n junction designed to operate in reverse breakdown (Zener breakdown). It maintains a nearly constant voltage across it (Zener voltage Vz) over a wide range of currents. It is used as a voltage regulator.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which logic gate is called a universal gate?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.8: Logic Gates
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.8: Logic Gates
questions.ncert_line: NOT gate: output is complement of input. AND gate: output is 1 only when all inputs are 1. OR gate: output is 0 only when all inputs are 0. NAND and NOR gates are universal gates from which all other gates can be constructed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The output of a NAND gate is LOW (0) only when:

options.C: One input is HIGH, other is LOW

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.8: Logic Gates
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.8: Logic Gates
questions.ncert_line: NOT gate: output is complement of input. AND gate: output is 1 only when all inputs are 1. OR gate: output is 0 only when all inputs are 0. NAND and NOR gates are universal gates from which all other gates can be constructed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: At absolute zero (0 K), a pure semiconductor behaves like a/an:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.4–14.5
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.4–14.5
questions.ncert_line: In intrinsic semiconductors, electrons and holes are always equal (ne = nh). In n-type (donor impurity), electrons are majority carriers. In p-type (acceptor impurity), holes are majority carriers. At 0 K, semiconductors behave as insulators.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who proposed the five kingdom classification of living organisms?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.1: Kingdom Monera
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.1: Kingdom Monera
questions.ncert_line: R.H. Whittaker proposed the five kingdom classification: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia. The basis of classification included: cell structure, body organisation, mode of nutrition, reproduction and phylogenetic relationships.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mycoplasmas are unique among bacteria because they:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.2.4: Mycoplasma
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.2.4: Mycoplasma
questions.ncert_line: Mycoplasmas are organisms that completely lack a cell wall. They are the smallest living cells known. They can survive without oxygen and are pathogenic in animals and plants.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Lichens are considered good indicators of air pollution.
Reason (R): Lichens are highly sensitive to SO₂ and other air pollutants and cannot survive in polluted air.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.4.5: Lichens
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.4.5: Lichens
questions.ncert_line: Lichens are symbiotic associations between algae (phycobiont) and fungi (mycobiont). The algal component is autotrophic and provides food; the fungal component provides shelter, absorbs water and minerals. Lichens are good pollution indicators.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statements about viruses is INCORRECT?

options.B: They may contain either DNA or RNA but never both
options.C: TMV was the first virus to be crystallised
options.D: All viruses have a protein coat and lipid envelope

questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.6: Viruses
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.6: Viruses
questions.ncert_line: Viruses are non-cellular entities that multiply only inside a living host cell. They have either DNA or RNA as genetic material, never both. They are acellular and obligate parasites. Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV) was the first virus to be crystallised by Wendell Stanley.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Prions differ from viroids in that prions:

options.A: Have single-stranded RNA as their genome
options.B: Are infectious protein particles with no nucleic acid
options.D: Require a host cell nucleus to replicate

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.6.2: Viroids and Prions
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.6.2: Viroids and Prions
questions.ncert_line: Viroids are free infectious RNA molecules without a protein coat, smaller than viruses. They cause diseases in plants. Prions are infectious protein particles (with no nucleic acid) that cause diseases like BSE (mad cow disease) and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease in humans.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The cell wall of fungi is composed of:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.4: Kingdom Fungi
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.4: Kingdom Fungi
questions.ncert_line: Fungi have cell walls made of chitin. They are heterotrophic (saprophytic, parasitic or symbiotic). The vegetative body is made of hyphae forming a mycelium. Asexual reproduction occurs by conidia, sporangiospores or budding.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly matches the algal class with its stored food?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.1: Algae
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.1: Algae
questions.ncert_line: Chlorophyceae (green algae) have chlorophyll a and b, starch as stored food, and flagella of equal length (isokont). Phaeophyceae (brown algae) have chlorophyll a, c, fucoxanthin, and mannitol/laminarin as stored food. Rhodophyceae (red algae) have r-phycoerythrin and floridean starch.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Pteridophytes are significant in plant evolution because they were the first plants to have:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.3: Pteridophytes
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.3: Pteridophytes
questions.ncert_line: Pteridophytes are the first terrestrial plants with vascular tissue (xylem and phloem). The main plant body is a sporophyte (diploid). Examples: Selaginella (heterosporous), Equisetum, Pteris, Nephrolepis. The gametophyte (prothallus) is small, inconspicuous and independent.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Animals that possess a true coelom (body cavity lined by mesoderm on all sides) are called:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.1: Basis of Classification
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.1: Basis of Classification
questions.ncert_line: The basis of classification of animals includes: levels of organisation (cellular, tissue, organ), symmetry (radial, bilateral), coelom (acoelomate, pseudocoelomate, coelomate), segmentation, notochord, and germinal layers (diploblastic, triploblastic).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The 'collar cells' (choanocytes) in sponges are responsible for:

options.C: Creating water current and capturing food

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.1: Phylum Porifera
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.1: Phylum Porifera
questions.ncert_line: Porifera (sponges) are diploblastic (two germinal layers), with a cellular level of organisation. They have a water canal system. Choanocytes (collar cells) line the spongocoel and canals. Body is supported by spicules or spongin fibres.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cnidoblasts (nematocysts) present in Coelenterata are used for:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.2: Phylum Coelenterata
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.2: Phylum Coelenterata
questions.ncert_line: Coelenterata (Cnidaria) are diploblastic with radial symmetry and tissue level of organisation. They have cnidoblasts (nematocytes) with stinging cells. They show alternation of generations between polyp (asexual) and medusa (sexual) forms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In Platyhelminthes, the excretion and osmoregulation is carried out by:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.3: Phylum Platyhelminthes
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.3: Phylum Platyhelminthes
questions.ncert_line: Platyhelminthes (flatworms) are triploblastic, acoelomate with bilateral symmetry and organ level of organisation. They are mostly endoparasites — Taenia (tapeworm), Fasciola (liver fluke). The flame cells help in osmoregulation and excretion.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The excretory organs in Annelida are:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.6: Phylum Annelida
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.6: Phylum Annelida
questions.ncert_line: Annelida are triploblastic, coelomate, bilaterally symmetrical and metamerically segmented. Excretion by nephridia. Earthworm (Lumbricus) is hermaphrodite. Polychaetes have parapodia for locomotion. Hirudinaria is a bloodsucking leech.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic feature of Arthropoda?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.7: Phylum Arthropoda
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.7: Phylum Arthropoda
questions.ncert_line: Arthropoda is the largest phylum with jointed appendages and exoskeleton of chitin. They have an open circulatory system. Excretion by Malpighian tubules (insects) or green glands (crustaceans). Examples: Locusta (grasshopper), Limulus (king crab), Apis (honeybee).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The unique locomotory and feeding structure in Echinoderms is the:

options.B: Tube feet (part of water vascular system)

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.10: Phylum Echinodermata
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2.10: Phylum Echinodermata
questions.ncert_line: Echinoderms are triploblastic, coelomate with radial symmetry (in adults) but bilateral symmetry in larvae. They have a unique water vascular system for locomotion. They have power of regeneration. Examples: Asterias (starfish), Echinus (sea urchin), Ophiura (brittle star).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which tissue provides mechanical strength to plants and has dead cells at maturity?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.1: Plant Tissues
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.1: Plant Tissues
questions.ncert_line: Meristematic tissues (apical, lateral, intercalary) have the ability to divide. Permanent tissues are either simple (parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma) or complex (xylem, phloem). Sclerenchyma provides mechanical strength — cells are dead at maturity.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following components of phloem is LIVING at maturity?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.1.3: Complex Tissues
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.1.3: Complex Tissues
questions.ncert_line: Xylem consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem fibres and xylem parenchyma. Vessels are dead at maturity and help in water conduction. Phloem consists of sieve tube elements, companion cells, phloem fibres and phloem parenchyma. Sieve tube elements are living.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Casparian strips in the endodermis of roots are made of:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.2: Anatomy of Dicotyledonous Root
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.2: Anatomy of Dicotyledonous Root
questions.ncert_line: The transverse section of a dicot root shows: epidermis (with root hairs), cortex, endodermis (with Casparian strips), pericycle, vascular bundles (xylem and phloem alternating, exarch). Pericycle gives rise to lateral roots.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The cork cambium (phellogen) produces cork (phellem) toward the outside and what toward the inside?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.3: Secondary Growth
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.3: Secondary Growth
questions.ncert_line: Secondary growth in dicot stems is brought about by two lateral meristems: vascular cambium (produces secondary xylem/wood and secondary phloem) and cork cambium (phellogen, produces cork/phellem outward and phelloderm inward).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is INCORRECT about a dorsiventral (dicot) leaf?

options.A: Palisade mesophyll is present on the upper (adaxial) side
options.B: Stomata are more numerous on the lower (abaxial) surface
options.C: Spongy mesophyll has loosely arranged cells with air spaces
options.D: Stomata are equally distributed on both surfaces

questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.2.4: Anatomy of Leaf
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants, Section 6.2.4: Anatomy of Leaf
questions.ncert_line: A dorsiventral (dicot) leaf has upper palisade mesophyll (compact, photosynthetically active) and lower spongy mesophyll (loosely arranged with air spaces). A dorsiventral leaf has more stomata on the lower (abaxial) surface.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a racemose inflorescence, the flowers are borne in:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 5 — Morphology of Flowering Plants, Section 5.4: The Inflorescence
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 5 — Morphology of Flowering Plants, Section 5.4: The Inflorescence
questions.ncert_line: The arrangement of flowers on the floral axis is called inflorescence. In a racemose inflorescence, the main axis grows continuously and flowers are borne laterally (acropetal succession). In a cymose inflorescence, the main axis terminates in a flower.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a typical flower, the androecium consists of:

options.C: Stamens, each with an anther and a filament

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 5 — Morphology of Flowering Plants, Section 5.5: The Flower
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 5 — Morphology of Flowering Plants, Section 5.5: The Flower
questions.ncert_line: A complete flower has all four whorls: calyx (sepals), corolla (petals), androecium (stamens) and gynoecium (pistil/carpels). The androecium consists of stamens (anther + filament). The gynoecium consists of one or more carpels (stigma + style + ovary).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of muscle is involuntary, striated, and branched?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 7 — Structural Organisation in Animals, Section 7.1: Animal Tissues
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 7 — Structural Organisation in Animals, Section 7.1: Animal Tissues
questions.ncert_line: The four types of animal tissues are: epithelial, connective, muscular and neural. Epithelial tissue forms the covering of body surfaces. Connective tissues have cells scattered in a matrix. Cardiac muscle is involuntary, striated and branched.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is INCORRECT about the cockroach (Periplaneta americana)?

options.A: It has an open circulatory system
options.C: It has a closed tracheal system for respiration
options.D: The fat body is the main metabolic and storage organ

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 7 — Structural Organisation in Animals, Section 7.3: Cockroach
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 7 — Structural Organisation in Animals, Section 7.3: Cockroach
questions.ncert_line: The cockroach (Periplaneta americana) has an open circulatory system. The fat body is the main metabolic organ. Excretion occurs via Malpighian tubules. Tracheal system is used for respiration. The compound eye can detect polarised light.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the fluid mosaic model, the plasma membrane consists of:

options.A: A lipid bilayer with protein molecules embedded in it
options.B: A protein bilayer with lipid molecules embedded in it
options.C: A single layer of lipids with proteins on the outside only
options.D: Equal amounts of carbohydrates and proteins

questions.explanation: Correct: A. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.3: Plasma Membrane
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.3: Plasma Membrane
questions.ncert_line: The fluid mosaic model (Singer and Nicolson, 1972) describes the plasma membrane as a fluid bilayer of phospholipids with proteins floating in it. Cholesterol is present between phospholipids and helps in maintaining membrane fluidity.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Lysosomes are called 'suicidal bags' of the cell because:

options.A: They produce ATP for cell death
options.B: They contain hydrolytic enzymes that can digest cellular contents
options.D: They release toxins to kill pathogens

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.6: Lysosomes
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.6: Lysosomes
questions.ncert_line: Lysosomes are membrane-bound vesicles containing hydrolytic enzymes (acid hydrolases). They digest foreign material (heterophagy) and worn-out organelles (autophagy). They are formed by the Golgi apparatus. They are called the 'suicidal bags' of the cell.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The dark reactions (Calvin cycle) of photosynthesis occur in which part of the chloroplast?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.8: Plastids
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.8: Plastids
questions.ncert_line: Chloroplasts are double-membrane organelles. The inner membrane is less permeable. The stroma contains thylakoids arranged in stacks (grana). Light reactions occur on the thylakoid membranes; dark reactions (Calvin cycle) occur in the stroma. Chloroplasts have their own DNA.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The nucleolus disappears during cell division. Its primary function in interphase is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.11: Nucleus
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.5.11: Nucleus
questions.ncert_line: The nucleus has a double membrane called the nuclear envelope with nuclear pores. The nucleoplasm contains chromatin (DNA + proteins). The nucleolus is the site of ribosomal RNA synthesis and ribosome assembly. It disappears during cell division.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is a free-living nitrogen-fixing bacterium found in soil?
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.4: Nitrogen Cycle
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.4: Nitrogen Cycle
questions.ncert_line: Biological nitrogen fixation is carried out by: free-living bacteria (Azotobacter, Clostridium), symbiotic bacteria (Rhizobium in root nodules of legumes), and cyanobacteria (Anabaena, Nostoc). The enzyme nitrogenase catalyses N₂ → NH₃. It requires strict anaerobic conditions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The enzyme responsible for biological nitrogen fixation is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.4: Nitrogen Cycle
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.4: Nitrogen Cycle
questions.ncert_line: Biological nitrogen fixation is carried out by: free-living bacteria (Azotobacter, Clostridium), symbiotic bacteria (Rhizobium in root nodules of legumes), and cyanobacteria (Anabaena, Nostoc). The enzyme nitrogenase catalyses N₂ → NH₃. It requires strict anaerobic conditions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Chlorosis (yellowing) appearing first in young leaves is typically caused by deficiency of:

options.A: Nitrogen (mobile — appears in old leaves first)
options.B: Potassium (mobile — appears in old leaves first)
options.C: Iron (immobile — appears in young leaves first)
options.D: Magnesium (mobile — appears in old leaves first)

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.3: Deficiency Symptoms of Essential Elements
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 12 — Mineral Nutrition, Section 12.3: Deficiency Symptoms of Essential Elements
questions.ncert_line: Iron (Fe) deficiency causes chlorosis (yellowing) in young leaves. Calcium (Ca) deficiency causes death of growing tips (necrosis of meristems). Potassium (K) deficiency causes chlorosis in older leaves first. Boron (B) deficiency causes death of terminal buds.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The cohesion-tension theory of ascent of sap was proposed by:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.2: Ascent of Sap
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.2: Ascent of Sap
questions.ncert_line: The cohesion-tension theory (Dixon and Joly) explains ascent of sap. Water molecules are held together by cohesion forces and to cell walls by adhesion. Transpiration from leaves creates tension (negative pressure) that pulls water up through xylem.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Transport in phloem is bidirectional (can move up or down).
Reason (R): Phloem transports sugars from source (leaf) to sink (roots/fruits) according to the pressure flow hypothesis.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.3: Phloem Transport
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.3: Phloem Transport
questions.ncert_line: The pressure flow hypothesis (Ernst Münch) explains phloem transport. High sugar concentration at source (leaf) creates high osmotic pressure; water moves in → high turgor pressure → pushes sap toward sink (root/fruit). The flow is bidirectional in phloem.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Short day plants flower when the duration of darkness exceeds a critical period. The photoreceptor involved in this response is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.5: Photoperiodism
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.5: Photoperiodism
questions.ncert_line: Photoperiodism is the response of plants to the relative duration of light and darkness. Short day plants (SDP) flower when the night period exceeds a critical length. Long day plants (LDP) flower when the night period is below the critical length. The photoreceptor is phytochrome.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Vernalisation refers to the promotion of flowering by:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.6: Vernalisation
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.6: Vernalisation
questions.ncert_line: Vernalisation is the acceleration of flowering by a period of low temperature. It is the cold treatment requirement for flowering in some plants (e.g., winter wheat, biennial plants like sugar beet). It prevents precocious reproductive development.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The net yield of ATP per glucose molecule in glycolysis is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.2–14.4
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.2–14.4
questions.ncert_line: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and produces 2 pyruvate, 2 ATP (net), and 2 NADH from one glucose molecule. Under aerobic conditions, pyruvate enters the mitochondria. The Krebs cycle produces 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 GTP per acetyl-CoA turn.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The respiratory quotient (RQ) for the complete oxidation of glucose (carbohydrate) is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.6: Respiratory Quotient
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.6: Respiratory Quotient
questions.ncert_line: Respiratory quotient (RQ) = CO₂ released / O₂ consumed. For carbohydrates, RQ = 1. For fats, RQ < 1 (about 0.7) as fats are more reduced and need more O₂. For organic acids, RQ > 1. RQ for succulent plants undergoing CAM is ≈ 0 at night.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following does NOT cause stomatal closure?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.3.3: Stomatal Transpiration
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.3.3: Stomatal Transpiration
questions.ncert_line: Stomata open when guard cells become turgid and close when they become flaccid. Stomatal opening is triggered by: light, low CO₂ concentration, and K⁺ accumulation in guard cells. ABA (abscisic acid) promotes stomatal closure.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which enzyme converts starch to maltose in the mouth?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.2–16.3
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.2–16.3
questions.ncert_line: Salivary amylase (ptyalin) digests starch to maltose. Pepsin in stomach digests proteins. Trypsin (from pancreas) digests proteins. Lipase (from pancreas) digests fats. Lactase digests lactose. The intestinal juice contains maltase, sucrase, lactase and peptidases.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: After absorption in intestinal cells, fats are transported via chylomicrons into the:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.4: Absorption of Digested Products
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.4: Absorption of Digested Products
questions.ncert_line: Glucose and amino acids are absorbed by active transport. Fatty acids and glycerol are absorbed by diffusion and re-synthesised into lipoproteins (chylomicrons) in intestinal cells. Fats enter the lymph (lacteals) via chylomicrons, NOT directly into the blood.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The normal RBC count in human blood is approximately:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.1: Blood
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.1: Blood
questions.ncert_line: Blood is composed of plasma (55%) and formed elements (45%). Plasma contains water (90-92%), proteins (fibrinogen, globulins, albumins). RBCs (erythrocytes) contain haemoglobin. Normal RBC count: 5 million/mm³. Platelets (thrombocytes) help in clotting.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The natural pacemaker of the human heart is the:
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.3: Heart
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.3: Heart
questions.ncert_line: The human heart has four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle. The right side pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs (pulmonary circuit). The left side pumps oxygenated blood to the body (systemic circuit). SA node is the pacemaker.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following distinguishes lymph from blood plasma?

options.C: Lymph has fewer proteins and no RBCs

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.6: Lymphatic System
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.6: Lymphatic System
questions.ncert_line: Lymph is a colourless fluid similar to plasma but with less proteins and no RBCs. The lymph nodes are present along lymphatic vessels. Lymph returns proteins and fluids to the blood. It carries chylomicrons (fat) absorbed from the intestine into the blood.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What percentage of the 180 litres of glomerular filtrate formed daily is reabsorbed back into the blood?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.2: Mechanism of Urine Formation
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.2: Mechanism of Urine Formation
questions.ncert_line: Urine formation involves three processes: ultrafiltration (at Bowman's capsule), selective reabsorption (along tubules), and tubular secretion. About 180 litres of filtrate forms per day, but only 1-2 litres of urine is excreted (99% reabsorption). ADH promotes water reabsorption.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) secretes renin in response to:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.4: Regulation of Kidney Function
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.4: Regulation of Kidney Function
questions.ncert_line: The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) is a specialised structure formed by the macula densa (modified cells of DCT) and juxtaglomerular cells (modified smooth muscle cells of afferent arteriole). JGA secretes renin when blood pressure falls, activating the renin-angiotensin system.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the sliding filament theory, muscle contraction is initiated when calcium ions bind to:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.2.2: Mechanism of Muscle Contraction
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.2.2: Mechanism of Muscle Contraction
questions.ncert_line: According to the sliding filament theory, muscle contraction occurs when actin filaments slide over myosin filaments. Cross bridges form between myosin heads and actin. ATP hydrolysis provides energy. Calcium ions (released from SR) initiate contraction by binding to troponin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The total number of bones in the adult human skeleton is:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.3: Skeletal System
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.3: Skeletal System
questions.ncert_line: The human skeleton has 206 bones. The axial skeleton (80 bones) includes skull, vertebral column, sternum and ribs. The appendicular skeleton (126 bones) includes limbs and girdles. Joints can be fibrous (immovable), cartilaginous (slightly movable) or synovial (freely movable).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which division of the autonomic nervous system is responsible for the 'fight or flight' response?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.4: Central Neural System
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.4: Central Neural System
questions.ncert_line: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the brain and spinal cord. The peripheral nervous system (PNS) consists of cranial and spinal nerves. The autonomic nervous system (ANS) has sympathetic (fight or flight) and parasympathetic (rest and digest) divisions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The semicircular canals in the inner ear are primarily responsible for:

options.D: Converting sound waves to nerve impulses

questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.3.2: Ear
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.3.2: Ear
questions.ncert_line: The human ear has three parts: outer ear (pinna and auditory canal), middle ear (tympanic membrane, malleus, incus, stapes), and inner ear (cochlea and vestibular apparatus). The cochlea is for hearing; semicircular canals are for balance.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which microorganism is used in the commercial production of acetic acid (vinegar)?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.1: Microbes in Household Products
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.1: Microbes in Household Products
questions.ncert_line: Lactobacillus and other lactic acid bacteria (LAB) help in curd formation — they convert lactose to lactic acid. Baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) is used in bread making. Aspergillus niger produces citric acid. Acetobacter aceti produces acetic acid (vinegar).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming from the fungus:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.2: Microbes in Industrial Products
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.2: Microbes in Industrial Products
questions.ncert_line: Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming from Penicillium notatum (now P. chrysogenum). It was the first antibiotic. Streptomycin is produced by Streptomyces griseus. Chloramphenicol is produced by Streptomyces venezuelae.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) is used to measure:

options.A: Amount of dissolved oxygen in water
options.B: Degree of organic pollution in water

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.5: Microbes in Sewage Treatment
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.5: Microbes in Sewage Treatment
questions.ncert_line: Primary treatment of sewage removes floating and suspended solid matter by filtration and sedimentation. Secondary (biological) treatment uses microbes to break down organic matter — the BOD (biochemical oxygen demand) is reduced. Activated sludge contains aerobic microbes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Baculoviruses are used as biocontrol agents because they are:

options.B: Species-specific pathogens that spare other organisms

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.3: Microbes in Biocontrol
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.3: Microbes in Biocontrol
questions.ncert_line: Baculoviruses (Nuclear Polyhedrosis Viruses — NPV) are species-specific pathogens used as biocontrol agents against insects and arthropods. Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is a biocontrol agent against butterfly caterpillars. Trichoderma is used to control plant fungal pathogens.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The main component of biogas produced in a biogas plant is:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.4: Microbes as Biofuel
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.4: Microbes as Biofuel
questions.ncert_line: Biogas (mainly methane) is produced by anaerobic bacteria (methanogens) from biomass. Methanobacterium is a methanogenic archaebacterium found in rumen of cattle. Biogas plants decompose cattle dung (gobar gas) anaerobically to produce biogas.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is essential in a cloning vector for identifying successful recombinants?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.2: Cloning Vectors
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.2: Cloning Vectors
questions.ncert_line: A cloning vector must have: an origin of replication (ori), a selectable marker (antibiotic resistance gene), and cloning sites (restriction enzyme recognition sites). Plasmids, bacteriophages (λ phage) and cosmids are commonly used vectors.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first recombinant human insulin (Humulin) was produced commercially in the bacterium:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.2: Genetically Engineered Insulin
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.2: Genetically Engineered Insulin
questions.ncert_line: Recombinant human insulin (Humulin) was the first commercially produced recombinant protein. It was produced in E. coli. The insulin gene was produced from two separate chains (A and B) with C-peptide linking them. Eli Lilly produced it commercially in 1982.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: RNA interference (RNAi) involves gene silencing using:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.3: RNA Interference
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.3: RNA Interference
questions.ncert_line: RNA interference (RNAi) is a mechanism of gene silencing using dsRNA (double-stranded RNA). Nematode-resistant tobacco was developed using RNAi. The dsRNA initiates RNAi in host cells, preventing translation of specific mRNA, providing protection against nematodes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Agrobacterium tumefaciens is used in plant genetic engineering because:

options.A: It can fix nitrogen in plant cells
options.B: Its Ti plasmid can transfer T-DNA into the plant genome
options.C: It produces antibiotics that select for transformed cells
options.D: It has restriction enzymes compatible with plant DNA

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.5: Agrobacterium as Vector
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.5: Agrobacterium as Vector
questions.ncert_line: Agrobacterium tumefaciens is a soil bacterium that causes crown gall disease in plants. Its Ti (tumour-inducing) plasmid can integrate a segment of its DNA (T-DNA) into the plant genome. It is used as a vector to introduce foreign genes into plant cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The final step in decomposition that releases inorganic nutrients from humus is called:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.3: Decomposition
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.3: Decomposition
questions.ncert_line: Decomposition is carried out by bacteria and fungi (decomposers) on dead organic matter (detritus). The steps are: fragmentation (by detritivores), leaching, catabolism, humification (dark-coloured humus formation) and mineralisation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The final stable community that develops at the end of ecological succession is called the:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.6: Ecological Succession
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.6: Ecological Succession
questions.ncert_line: Primary succession begins in bare areas (bare rock, pond, sand dune). The pioneer community modifies the environment making it suitable for more complex communities. Each successive community is called a seral community. The final stable community is the climax community.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: India has three biodiversity hotspots. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4.2: Hotspots
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4.2: Hotspots
questions.ncert_line: Biodiversity hotspots are regions with high levels of endemic species and high levels of habitat loss. India has three hotspots: the Western Ghats and Sri Lanka, the Eastern Himalayas, and the Indo-Burma region.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is an ex situ conservation method?
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4: Conservation of Biodiversity
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4: Conservation of Biodiversity
questions.ncert_line: In situ conservation means conserving species in their natural habitats (national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, biosphere reserves). Ex situ conservation is off-site conservation in zoos, botanical gardens, seed banks and cryopreservation. Seed banks preserve seeds at −196°C (liquid nitrogen).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the logistic growth model, the population growth rate is maximum when the population size N equals:
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.2.1: Growth Models
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.2.1: Growth Models
questions.ncert_line: Population growth can be exponential (J-shaped, dN/dt = rN) or logistic (S-shaped, dN/dt = rN(K−N)/K). The logistic growth has a carrying capacity K. The inflection point of logistic growth is at N = K/2 where growth rate is maximum.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The international agreement to phase out CFC production to protect the ozone layer is called the:
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.5: Ozone Depletion
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.5: Ozone Depletion
questions.ncert_line: Ozone (O₃) in the stratosphere absorbs harmful UV-B radiation. CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) released from refrigerants and aerosols destroy the ozone layer. The ozone hole is most prominent over Antarctica. The Montreal Protocol (1987) banned CFCs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Eutrophication refers to:

options.A: Depletion of oxygen in water bodies
options.B: Excessive nutrient enrichment causing algal bloom in water bodies
options.C: Accumulation of heavy metals in water

questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.2: Water Pollution
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.2: Water Pollution
questions.ncert_line: The full form of BOD is Biochemical Oxygen Demand — it is the amount of oxygen needed by bacteria to decompose organic matter in water. High BOD indicates high organic pollution. Eutrophication is excessive growth of phytoplankton due to enrichment of nutrients in water bodies.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which nutrient cycle does NOT have a significant atmospheric component?
